event: The Hospitality of Abraham (Old Testament Trinity)
symbols: three angels seated equally — the co-equal Persons of the Holy Trinity: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit
chalice on the table — the Eucharist, the sacrificial offering of Christ, the Lamb slain before the foundation of the world
oak tree — the Oak of Mamre where Abraham received the three visitors
building (left) — Abraham's house or the Temple, representing the Father's house
mountain (right) — the Holy Spirit's association with the heights of divine revelation
circular composition of the three figures — the eternal perichoresis (mutual indwelling) of the Trinity
each figure holding a staff — authority and divine pilgrimage
description: Rublev's iconic depiction of three angels seated around a table bearing a chalice. The figures are arranged in a gentle circular composition against a gold background. The left angel wears golden-pink robes, the central angel wears dark brown and blue, and the right angel wears green and blue. Each has a halo and holds a staff. All three incline their heads gently. Behind them, from left to right: a building, a tree, and a rocky mountain. The chalice at center holds a small form. The color palette is harmonious with blues, golds, and greens predominating.
theological_description: Rublev's Trinity icon is arguably the most profound visual expression of Trinitarian theology in the history of Christian art. Based on the Genesis account of three visitors received by Abraham at the Oak of Mamre (GEN.18.1-8), it transcends the narrative to reveal the eternal communion of Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. The three figures are identical in dignity yet distinct in gesture and color — expressing the Cappadocian formula of three Persons in one essence. Their circular arrangement embodies the concept of perichoresis: the mutual indwelling and interpenetration of the divine Persons. The chalice at center contains the sacrificial lamb — the Eucharistic offering that proceeds from the eternal counsel of the Trinity, 'the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world' (REV.13.8). The open space at the front of the table invites the viewer into the divine communion — the Trinity is not a closed circle but an open embrace. The building, tree, and mountain behind each figure have been interpreted as representing the Father's house (JHN.14.2), the tree of life restored through the Cross, and the mountain of the Spirit's revelation.

scripture_refs.reason: Abraham's hospitality to the three visitors at the Oak of Mamre — the narrative source of the icon
The Trinitarian formula — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — visually expressed in the three co-equal figures
In my Father's house are many rooms — the building behind the left figure
The Lamb slain from the foundation of the world — the chalice at the center of the Trinity's counsel
That they may all be one, as you Father are in me and I in you — the perichoretic communion
